skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automate browser sessions for AI agents with inference.sh. Open pages, inspect element refs, interact with forms, capture screenshots or video, run page JavaScript, upload files, and route sessions through proxies.
skill.summary: Browser automation for AI agents using inference.sh and compact element refs.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, filesystem, and credential findings are mostly expected for a browser automation skill and many are documentation examples. However, the skill explicitly supports authenticated browsing, cookie extraction guidance, proxy rotation for rate-limit avoidance, screenshots, video recording, JavaScript execution, and local file uploads. No prompt injection attempt was found, but the community skill should not be published without manual review and stronger safety warnings.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Cookie Extraction Guidance
high_findings.description: The authentication reference includes a section for extracting cookies from an authenticated browser session and printing them. This is a real credential exposure risk because session cookies can grant account access if copied or logged.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly names cookie extraction and shows the result being assigned and echoed. This is strong semantic evidence of sensitive session data handling, even if presented as a legitimate workflow.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation for Rate Limit Avoidance
high_findings.description: The proxy support reference documents rotating proxies for web scraping and labels the workflow as rate limit avoidance. This can enable abusive scraping, evasion of site controls, or policy violations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section title and example workflow directly connect proxy rotation with scraping and rate limit avoidance. Legitimate testing is possible, but the abuse potential is clear.

high_findings.title: Anonymous SOCKS Proxy Browsing
high_findings.description: The proxy guide supports privacy-focused and SOCKS5 proxy routing. This is useful for testing, but it can also hide automation origin and complicate abuse detection.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence clearly documents SOCKS5 and privacy proxy use. The feature is legitimate for network testing, so confidence is high but below malicious certainty.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
medium_findings.description: The command reference exposes an execute function that runs caller-provided JavaScript in the browser page. This is expected for automation, but it can extract page content, click hidden elements, or manipulate authenticated pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute capability is explicitly documented with a code field. The execution context is the page, not the local host, which lowers the severity from critical.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Screenshot and Video Capture
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and record all browser activity during a session. The documentation notes that recordings may include credentials or sensitive application data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recording capability and sensitive-data warning are both explicit. This is a real privacy risk, but the documentation also includes mitigation guidance.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The interact function accepts local file paths for upload actions. A careless or malicious workflow could upload unintended local files through a web page.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file_paths field and upload action are clearly documented. The risk depends on user-selected paths and target pages, so confidence is moderate-high.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling in Shell Templates
medium_findings.description: The authenticated-session template reads username and password environment variables and sends them through browser automation commands. This is useful, but it places credential safety on the caller and shell environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template avoids hardcoding credentials, which is positive. It still handles live credentials in shell commands, so accidental logging or process exposure remains possible.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Findings Are Mostly Intended Usage
low_findings.description: Most static external command findings are markdown command examples or helper templates that call the expected inference.sh browser CLI. They are not hidden local command execution inside the skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show documented CLI usage and shell templates, not concealed execution. The commands are still operational, so they remain a low risk factor.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Primarily Examples
low_findings.description: The static hardcoded URL findings are mainly example domains and documentation links. They do not show exfiltration endpoints or unauthorized callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are examples used to demonstrate inputs. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or telemetry from the skill.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Several path traversal alerts point to markdown links that reference nearby documentation. These are documentation navigation links, not filesystem reads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are plain markdown references using relative paths. No evidence found of code that reads arbitrary parent directories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary URL and Proxy-Controlled Browser Sessions
dangerous_patterns.description: The open function accepts a target URL and optional proxy settings. This creates broad network reach from the browser automation environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The input fields and examples directly support arbitrary navigation and proxy credentials. This is an intended but sensitive browser automation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function runs supplied JavaScript against the active page and returns results with a screenshot.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented code parameter is direct evidence. The impact is scoped to the browser page context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Screen Capture and Recording
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill captures screenshots and can record full browser sessions, including dynamic content and user interactions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capture behavior is explicitly documented. The content sensitivity depends on the visited page and workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Upload Through Browser Forms
dangerous_patterns.description: The upload action accepts file paths and sends files through page file inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The feature is explicit, but exploitation depends on caller intent and selected file paths.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows With Agent Browser
content.value_statement: AI agents often need to inspect and operate real web pages, but raw browser automation is verbose. Agent Browser gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compact element refs, screenshots, videos, and scripted workflows through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Opens browser sessions and returns compact element refs for interactive page elements.
Clicks, fills, types, selects, checks, scrolls, drags, uploads files, and navigates by URL.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots and optional session videos.
Runs JavaScript in the active page for extraction, inspection, or interaction.
Supports authenticated workflows, session reuse patterns, and proxy configuration.
Provides shell templates for forms, authenticated sessions, and content capture.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and access to the agent-browser service.
Element refs can become stale after navigation or dynamic page changes.
Browser outputs may contain sensitive page data, credentials, screenshots, or recordings.
The skill does not verify permission to automate or scrape target websites.

use_cases.title: Test Web Flows
use_cases.description: Open pages, interact with controls, verify results, and capture screenshots or videos for test evidence.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Visit public pages, extract visible text and links, and save screenshots for source review.

use_cases.title: Document Product Workflows
use_cases.description: Record guided browser sessions and capture key screens for help articles or release notes.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target URL with Agent Browser. Summarize the page title, visible interactive elements, and the safest next action.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Simple Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Agent Browser to open this form, identify the relevant refs, fill the provided fields, submit it, and report the final page state.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Research Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to these public pages, capture full-page screenshots, extract visible text and links, then close every browser session.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Authenticated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an authenticated browser workflow using environment-provided credentials. Avoid recording sensitive screens, re-snapshot after navigation, and close the session when finished.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and identify the primary actions.
output_examples.output: The browser opens the page, reports the title, lists buttons and form controls with refs, and recommends the next interaction.

output_examples.input: Capture a public article for review.
output_examples.output: The workflow saves a full-page screenshot, extracts visible text and links, and closes the browser session.
The agent re-snapshots the page, reports validation messages, captures a screenshot, and explains which ref should be retried.

best_practices: Always re-snapshot after navigation, form submission, modal changes, or dynamic content updates.
Close every browser session after the workflow to reduce data exposure and resource use.
Avoid recording or logging pages that contain passwords, tokens, personal data, or private customer information.
anti_patterns: Do not use proxy rotation to bypass rate limits, blocks, or website access controls.
Do not upload local files unless the exact file path and target page are verified.
Do not print cookies, passwords, screenshots, or videos into shared logs or chat transcripts.

faq.question: What does Agent Browser automate?
faq.answer: It opens web pages, returns compact element refs, performs interactions, captures screenshots, records videos, and runs page JavaScript.

faq.question: Does it require another service?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires the inference.sh CLI and the agent-browser service that runs the browser automation.

faq.question: Can it log in to websites?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes authenticated workflow examples. Users must protect credentials and follow target site rules.

faq.question: Can it scrape websites?
faq.answer: It can extract visible text, links, and page data. Users must respect robots rules, terms, and legal limits.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It can handle credentials, cookies, screenshots, videos, proxies, local file uploads, and arbitrary page JavaScript.

faq.question: Is it safe to record browser sessions?
faq.answer: Record only non-sensitive workflows. Videos can capture private content, credentials, and customer data.
